# EDA [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#EDA)

### Image resolution [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Image-resolution)

You should examine the distribution of image sizes in the dataset, for at least a few reasons.

1. **Impact on model performance**: Image size can significantly impact the performance of object detection models. Models trained on smaller images may have difficulty detecting small objects. In comparison, models trained on larger images may require more computational resources and training time. Understanding the distribution of image sizes in the dataset can help select an appropriate model architecture and hyperparameters.

2. **Memory constraints**: Training object detection models on large images can require a lot of memory, which may be a limitation for some hardware configurations. Suppose the image sizes in the dataset are too large. In that case, resizing or cropping the images may be necessary before training the model, which can affect the model's performance.

3. **Generalizability**: Object detection models are typically trained on a specific range of image sizes and may not perform well on images outside that range. Suppose the test images have different sizes than the training images. In that case, the model may not detect objects accurately. Understanding the range of image sizes in the dataset can help select appropriate data augmentation techniques and evaluate the model's performance on test images.


### Plot some images with bboxes [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Plot-some-images-with-bboxes)

Visualizing some sample images from the dataset can give an initial understanding of the type of images in the dataset, the background, and the object of interest.

This can help decide whether the dataset is appropriate for the task at hand and identify any potential issues such as low-quality images, missing annotations, or class imbalance. Visualizing some sample images with bounding boxes can help understand the annotations' quality and the distribution of the objects in the images.

This can also help identify potential issues such as misaligned bounding boxes or overlapping objects.

### Distribution of classes [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Distribution-of-classes)

Understanding the distribution of classes in the dataset is important to identify class imbalance and the prevalence of rare classes. This information can be used to adjust the loss function or the sampling strategy to give more weight to the rare classes during training.

### How many detections are in each picture [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#How-many-detections-are-in-each-picture)

Understanding the number of detections (bounding boxes) in each image can give an idea of the complexity of the task and the level of detail required for the model to perform well.

This can also help identify any annotation issues, such as missing or redundant bounding boxes.

## Image resolutions [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Image-resolutions)

The following code outputs a set of unique resolutions (width and height) for a list of images.

This code uses the OpenCV library to read images from a list of image paths (`image_paths`), extract their resolution (width and height), and append it to a list called `resolutions`.

Then, the code creates a set of unique resolutions from the resolutions list using the `set()` function. A set is a collection of unique elements, so any duplicate resolutions in the list will be removed from the set.

It then prints the unique resolutions so you can quickly examine what you're working with.

In \[7\]:

linkcode

```
resolutions = []

for image_path in image_paths:
    img = cv2.imread(str(image_path))
    h, w, _ = img.shape
    resolutions.append((w, h))

unique_resolutions = set(resolutions)
print("Unique resolutions:", unique_resolutions)

## Class distribution [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Class-distribution)

The following code outputs a bar chart showing the class distribution in a dataset based on the given label files.

This code calculates the class distribution of a dataset given a list of label files (`label_paths`). Each label file contains a list of object annotations, where each line consists of a class ID and the coordinates of the object in the image.

First, the code creates a Counter object `class_counts` to keep track of the number of instances for each class ID. It then iterates over each label file, reads the lines, extracts the class ID from each line, and updates the class\_counts object with the count of each class ID.

Next, the code replaces the class IDs with class names using a list of class names called `class_names`, and creates a new dictionary `class_counts_names` with the class names as keys and their corresponding counts from class\_counts as values.

After that, the code creates a pandas DataFrame from the `class_counts_names` dictionary and plots a bar chart using `matplotlib`.

The x-axis represents the class names, the y-axis represents the number of instances for each class, and the plot title is "Class Distribution."

In \[8\]:

linkcode

```
class_counts = Counter()
for label_file in label_paths:
    with open(label_file, "r") as file:
        lines = file.readlines()
    class_counts.update(Counter([int(line.split()[0]) for line in lines]))

# Replace class IDs with class names
class_counts_names = {class_names[int(class_id)]: count for class_id, count in class_counts.items()}

# Create a pandas DataFrame and plot the bar chart
df = pd.DataFrame.from_dict(class_counts_names, orient="index", columns=["count"])
ax = df.plot(kind="bar")
plt.xlabel("Classes")
plt.ylabel("Number of Instances")
plt.title("Class Distribution")
plt.show()
```

# Distribution of detections and classes across all images [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Distribution-of-detections-and-classes-across-all-images)

The following code outputs a pandas DataFrame with columns `file`, `num_lines`, and `unique_values`, where each row represents a label file and its corresponding number of lines and unique values.

This code creates a pandas DataFrame containing the number of lines and unique values (class IDs) for each label file in a given list of label paths (`label_paths`).

The code first initializes an empty list called data. Then, it iterates over each file path in label\_paths and reads the contents of the file using `open()`.

For each file, it reads the lines using `readlines()` function and counts the number of lines with `len(lines)`.

It also counts the number of unique values (class IDs) in the file by first extracting the class IDs from each line using `map(lambda x: x.split()[0], lines)` which returns a list of class IDs.

Then it converts the list of class IDs to a set to remove duplicates, and gets the length of the set using `len(set())`.

And finally, the code appends the `file`, `num_lines`, and `unique_values` to the data list, and creates a pandas DataFrame using `pd.DataFrame()` with data as the first argument and a list of column names as the columns argument.

It then plots these values using pandas built-in plotting methods.

In \[9\]:

linkcode

```
data = []

for file in label_paths:
    with open(file) as f:
        lines = f.readlines()
        num_lines = len(lines)
        unique_values = len(set(list(map(lambda x: x.split()[0], lines))))

        data.append([file, num_lines, unique_values])

df = pd.DataFrame(data, columns=['file', 'num_lines', 'unique_values'])
```

# Plot some images with their bounding boxes [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Plot-some-images-with-their-bounding-boxes)

The following code outputs a randomly selected set of images with bounding boxes and class labels based on the corresponding label files.

It starts by randomly selects a specified number of images from a directory (`images_dir`), reads the corresponding label files, and displays the images with bounding boxes and class labels. It also defines the number of images to randomly select (`num_images`) and getting a list of all image files in the images\_dir directory using a list comprehension.

Then, it shuffles the list of image files randomly using the `random.shuffle()` function selects the specified number of images using slicing and stores them in the `selected_image_files list`.

Next, each selected image file reads the corresponding label file using `pathlib.Path()` and `open()` function and processes each line in the label file.

For each line, it extracts the class ID, colour, bounding box coordinates, and class name from the line.

It draws a rectangle and a filled rectangle for the bounding box and class label using the OpenCV `cv2.rectangle()` function.

It also writes the class name on the image using the `cv2.putText()` function.

After all that it shows the image using `matplotlib.pyplot.imshow()` function with the bounding boxes and class labels drawn on it. It also optionally resizes the image using `cv2.resize()` function (currently commented out).

In \[12\]:

linkcode

```
# Number of images to randomly select
num_images = 5

# Get the list of all image files in the 'images' directory
image_files = [f for f in pathlib.Path(images_dir).iterdir() if f.is_file()]

# Shuffle the list of image files
random.shuffle(image_files)

# Select the specified number of image files
selected_image_files = image_files[:num_images]

for selected_image_file in selected_image_files:
    demo_image = selected_image_file
    # Get the corresponding label file
    demo_label = pathlib.Path(labels_dir) / f"{selected_image_file.stem}.txt"

    # Load the image using OpenCV's imread function
    image = cv2.imread(str(demo_image))

    # Get the list of class names from the 'data' dictionary
    class_list = data_yaml['names']

    # Define a list of colors to be used to draw bounding boxes
    colors = [(255, 0, 0), (0, 255, 0), (0, 0, 255)]

    # Get the height and width of the image
    height, width, _ = image.shape

    # Create an empty list T
    T = []

    # Open the label file 'demo_label' in read mode and process each line
    with open(demo_label, "r") as file1:
        for line in file1.readlines():
            # Split the line into a list of strings
            split = line.split(" ")

            # Get the class id from the first element of the split list
            class_id = int(split[0])

            # Get the color corresponding to the class id from the 'colors' list
            color = colors[class_id]
            clazz = class_list[class_id]

            # Get the x, y, w, h bounding box coordinates from the split list
            x, y, w, h = float(split[1]), float(split[2]), float(split[3]), float(split[4])

            # Rescale the x, y, w, h values to the size of the image
            box = [int((x - 0.5*w)* width), int((y - 0.5*h) * height), int(w*width), int(h*height)]

            # Draw a rectangle on the image using the 'box' and 'color' values
            cv2.rectangle(image, box, color, 2)

            # Draw a filled rectangle for the class label on the image
            cv2.rectangle(image, (box[0], box[1] - 20), (box[0] + box[2], box[1]), color, -1)

            # Write the class label on the image
            cv2.putText(image, class_list[class_id], (box[0], box[1] - 5), cv2.FONT_HERSHEY_SIMPLEX, .5, (0,0,0))

        # Show the image using matplotlib
        plt.imshow(cv2.cvtColor(image, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB))
        # Optionally resize the image (commented out in code)
        image = cv2.resize(image, (600, 600))

# Baseline [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Baseline)

### Let's talk about learning rate and learning rate schedulers [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Let's-talk-about-learning-rate-and-learning-rate-schedulers)

The learning rate is a key hyperparameter in object detection and other machine learning tasks that determines the step size taken during optimization to update the model's parameters.

The learning rate controls how quickly or slowly the model learns from the training data and is important in achieving good performance. In object detection, using a decreasing learning rate schedule is common, where the learning rate starts with an initial value and gradually decreases over time. Two important hyperparameters are related to this schedule: the initial and final learning rates.

The initial learning rate determines the size of the first step taken during optimization.

A higher initial learning rate can lead to faster convergence and training time. On the flip side, it might result in unstable optimization and poor performance if the learning rate is too high. A lower initial learning rate may result in slower convergence and longer training time, but still might lead to more stable optimization and better performance.

The final learning rate determines the minimum value that the learning rate can reach during training.

This is important to prevent the learning rate from becoming too small and slowing down the convergence of the model. A too-small final learning rate can lead to the model getting stuck in suboptimal solutions and taking too long to converge.

### Learning rate schedulers [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Learning-rate-schedulers)

It's common to use a learning rate schedule that starts with a relatively high initial learning rate and gradually decreases to a lower final learning rate over time. This can help to balance the trade-off between fast convergence and stable optimization while preventing the learning rate from becoming too small.

The cosine learning rate scheduler is a schedule that adjusts the learning rate over time based on a cosine function.

The basic idea behind the cosine learning rate scheduler is to gradually decrease the learning rate over time smoothly and continuously. This is achieved by multiplying the initial learning rate by a cosine function that varies between 1 and 0 throughout training. The cosine function is defined as:

`lr = 0.5 * (1 + cos(epoch / epochs * pi)) * initial_lr`

Where epoch is the current epoch number, epochs is the total number of epochs, pi is the mathematical constant pi, and initial\_lr is the initial learning rate.

At the beginning of training, the cosine function takes the value of 1, so the learning rate equals the initial learning rate. As training progresses, the cosine function gradually decreases, causing the learning rate to decrease as well. The learning rate reaches its minimum value (i.e., zero) at the end of training.

The cosine learning rate scheduler has several advantages over other learning rate schedules.

1. Smooth and continuous function that can help avoid sudden changes in the learning rate that can disrupt the optimization process.

2. Dood balance between fast convergence and stability, which can help improve the model's performance.

3. Easy to implement and requires minimal hyperparameter tuning.


## Baseline configuration [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Baseline-configuration)

For a baseline model we'll use the pre-trained YOLOv8 with the following configuration:

- Change image size to 416 to correspond to the size of images we have
- Use the Adam optimizer
- Train for 25 epochs
- Use a pretrained model
- Change initial and final learning rate
- Use the Cosine learnig rate scheduler

We'll keep the remainder of the configuration the same the [YOLOv8 defaults](https://github.com/ultralytics/ultralytics/blob/177a68b39ffe7f87c5bc17a78fa5322af9f77c0b/ultralytics/yolo/cfg/default.yaml) and modify them one at a time as we progress through the notebook.

# Understanding the results [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Understanding-the-results)

This output shows the performance of the YOLOv8 model on the validation dataset. Specifically, it shows the precision, recall, and mAP (mean average precision) scores for each class and all combined classes.

Let's breakdown what we're seeing here:

`Class:` This column shows the name of the class being evaluated. The model is evaluated on Platelets, RBC, and WBC in this example.

`Images`: This column shows the number of images in the test dataset containing at least one instance of the evaluated class.

`Instances`: This column shows the total number of class instances in the test dataset.

`Box(P)`: This column shows the precision score for the bounding boxes of the class. Precision measures the fraction of predicted bounding boxes that are correct.

`Box(R)`: This column shows the recall score for the bounding boxes of the class. Recall measures the fraction of true bounding boxes detected by the model.

`mAP50`: This column shows the mean average precision (mAP) score at a threshold of 50%. mAP is a common metric used to evaluate the performance of object detection models.

`mAP50-95`: This column shows the mAP score over a range of thresholds from 50% to 95%.

Object detection aims to achieve high precision and recall scores, indicating that the model is accurately detecting and localizing the objects in the image. The mAP score provides an overall measure of the model's performance, considering precision and recall. A higher mAP score indicates better performance.

# Changing more parmeters [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Changing-more-parmeters)

Now let's take the trained baseline model and tweak some of the default settings.

In this iteration, let's enable label smoothing. Label smoothing is a regularization technique commonly used in deep learning to prevent overfitting and improve the model's generalization. In object detection, label smoothing can improve the model's performance by reducing the effect of noisy or incorrect annotations.

In object detection, each object in an image is labelled with a bounding box and a class label.

The class label is typically a one-hot encoded vector. One element is set to 1 to indicate the presence of the corresponding object class, and all other elements are set to 0. However, the one-hot encoding assumes that the labels are completely accurate, which may not be the case in practice due to human annotation errors or ambiguity in the data.

Label smoothing addresses this issue by replacing the one-hot encoding with a smoothed distribution over the classes.

Instead of assigning a value of 1 to the true class label and 0 to all other labels, the smoothed distribution assigns a small non-zero value (e.g., 0.1) to the true class label and a small non-zero value (e.g., 0.05) to all other labels. This has treduceseffect of noisy or incorrect annotations, as the model is encouraged to be less confident about the training labels and more robust to small changes in the input. The benefits of label smoothing for object detection include improved accuracy and generalization and reduced sensitivity to the choice of hyperparameters, such as learning rate and weight decay.

There is a tradeoff: label smoothing can also reduce the model's ability to fit the training data precisely. Use it judiciously and with other regularization techniques such as dropout and weight decay.

# Augmentations [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Augmentations)

When working with blood cell images, it's essential to choose augmentations that preserve the characteristics of the cells while increasing the model's robustness to variations in the dataset.

`hsv_h`: 0.02. Based on the training data, the hue of the blood cells can vary slightly, so a slightly larger fraction for hue augmentation can help to introduce more variation in the colour of the blood cells.

`hsv_s`: 0.5. The saturation of the blood cells appears to vary significantly in the training data, so a moderate fraction for saturation augmentation can help to introduce more contrast and make the blood cells more visible.

`hsv_v`: 0.3. The brightness of the blood cells varies slightly in the training data, so a moderate fraction for value augmentation can help to adjust the brightness and enhance the contrast between the blood cells and the background.

`degrees`: 5.0. The blood cells are mostly oriented in a horizontal or vertical direction in the training data, so a small rotation of up to 5 degrees can help to introduce some variation in the orientation of the blood cells.

`translate`: 0.05. Based on the training data, the blood cells can vary in position within the image, so a small fraction for translation can help to introduce more variation while keeping the blood cells within the same image.

`scale`: 0.3. The size of the blood cells varies significantly in the training data, so a moderate gain for scaling can help adjust the blood cells' size and introduce more variation.

`shear`: 0.0. The blood cells appear mostly circular in the training data, so shearing the images may not be necessary.

`perspective`: 0.0. The blood cells appear to be viewed from a fixed angle in the training data, so introducing perspective distortion may not be necessary.

`flipud`: 0.0. Flipping the images upside down may not be necessary for blood cell images, as the cells are typically viewed from a specific orientation.

`fliplr`: 0.3. Flipping the images horizontally can help to introduce more variation while keeping the orientation of the blood cells consistent.

`mosaic`: 0.5. Mosaic augmentation can help introduce more variation by combining multiple images into one image. However, using it too frequently may introduce too much variation and make the model less robust to the data.

`mixup`: 0.0. Mixup augmentation may not be necessary for blood cell images, as the cells are typically homogeneous and do not have distinct features. Mixing the images may introduce unnecessary variation that may be detrimental to the model's performance.

# Focal Loss [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Focal-Loss)

Focal loss gamma is a hyperparameter that controls the degree to which the focal loss function focuses on hard examples during training.

Focal loss is commonly used in object detection and other computer vision tasks, and is designed to address the problem of class imbalance in the training data. Like our situation where the Platelet and WBC classes have far fewer examples than the RBC examples. In situations like this the model may struggle to learn to recognize those under represented classes.

How does the focal loss work?

It modifies the standard cross-entropy loss function by introducing a tunable parameter `gamma`, which controls the rate at which the contribution of easy examples to the loss is reduced. Specifically, the focal loss function assigns a higher weight to hard examples (i.e., misclassified with high confidence) and a lower weight to easy examples (i.e., correctly classified with high confidence).

The gamma parameter controls the rate at which the weight assigned to easy examples is reduced.

A higher gamma value (e.g., 2) reduces the weight assigned to easy examples more quickly, making the loss more focused on hard examples. A lower gamma value (e.g., 0.5) gradually reduces the weight assigned to easy examples, making the loss less focused on hard examples.

The value for gamma is typically choose a value by cross-validation or grid search, since it can have a significant impact on the performance of the model.

For this example, we will set the value to be

# The `box`, `cls` and `dfl` hyperparameters [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#The-box,-cls-and-dfl-hyperparameters)

In the context of object detection these parameters control the relative weights of different components of the loss function used for training the model.

The loss function is used to measure the difference between the predicted output of the model and the ground-truth labels and to update the model parameters during training.

`box`: This parameter controls the **weight given to the localization loss in the loss function**. It measures the **difference between the predicted bounding box coordinates and the ground-truth bounding box coordinates**. Higher values emphasize accurate object localization, which is particularly important for object detection.

`cls`: This parameter controls the **weight given to the classification loss in the loss function**. It **measures the difference between the predicted and ground-truth class probabilities.** Higher values emphasize accurate classification of the object, which is also important for object detection.

`dfl`: This parameter controls the **weight given to the distance-based focal loss (DFL) in the loss function**. DFL is a variant of the focal loss that **weights the loss based on the distance between the predicted and ground-truth bounding boxes**. Higher values emphasize accurate localization. It can help to reduce false positives and false negatives in object detection.

Tuning these parameters can help improve the model's performance and achieve better results in object detection tasks.

For example, a higher value of the box loss gain may be necessary if the objects in the dataset are small and require precise localization. On the other hand, a higher value of the cls loss gain may be necessary if the objects are similar in appearance and require accurate classification. The dfl loss gain may also need to be adjusted depending on the level of false positives and false negatives in the dataset.

## Choosing values for the box, cls, and dfl loss gains in the context of blood cell detection: [¶](https://www.kaggle.com/code/harpdeci/blood-cell-detection-with-yolov8\#Choosing-values-for-the-box,-cls,-and-dfl-loss-gains-in-the-context-of-blood-cell-detection:)

`box: 10.0.` Blood cells are relatively small and require precise localization, so a higher value for the box loss gain can help to ensure that the model accurately localizes the cells.

`cls: 2.0.` Blood cells can be relatively homogeneous in appearance, so a higher value for the cls loss gain can help to ensure that the model accurately classifies the cells.

`dfl: 2.0`. False positives and false negatives can be particularly problematic in the context of blood cell detection, so a higher value for the dfl loss gain can help to reduce these errors and improve the overall performance of the model.

These values are for demonstration purposes, and you should tune them in practice. These values are also affected by other factors such as the learning rate and data augmentation techniques. You should experiment with different values and techniques to find the best combination, and I will leave that as homework for you.

web.markdown:
# Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN

Report issue for preceding element

Siddharth Sahay

Report issue for preceding element

###### Abstract

Report issue for preceding element

This paper presents a comprehensive methodology and comparative performance analysis for the automated classification and object detection of peripheral blood cells (PBCs) in microscopic images. Addressing the critical challenge of data scarcity and heterogeneity, robust data pipeline was first developed to standardize and merge four public datasets (PBC, BCCD, Chula, Sickle Cell) into a unified resource. Then employed a state-of-the-art Faster R-CNN object detection framework, leveraging a ResNet-50-FPN backbone. Comparative training rigorously evaluated a randomly initialized baseline model (Regimen 1) against a Transfer Learning Regimen (Regimen 2), initialized with weights pre-trained on the Microsoft COCO dataset. The results demonstrate that the Transfer Learning approach achieved significantly faster convergence and superior stability, culminating in a final validation loss of 0.08666, a substantial improvement over the baseline. This validated methodology establishes a robust foundation for building high-accuracy, deployable systems for automated hematological diagnosis.

Report issue for preceding element

## I Introduction

Report issue for preceding element

Peripheral Blood Smear (PBS) analysis is a foundational procedure in medical diagnostics, enabling the identification and quantification of various blood cell types, including White Blood Cells (WBCs), Red Blood Cells (RBCs), and platelets\[ [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib6 "")\]. The accuracy of this process is crucial for detecting hematological disorders such as anemia, leukemia, and infections. Traditionally, this analysis relies on labor-intensive and subjective manual microscopic examination, necessitating highly skilled personnel \[ [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib2 "")\]. This dependence introduces variability, fatigue-related errors, and scalability challenges.

Report issue for preceding element

The advent of Deep Learning (DL) and Computer Vision techniques offers a powerful paradigm shift toward automated, objective, and high-throughput hematological analysis. Specifically, object detection models, such as the Faster R-CNN architecture \[ [10](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib10 "")\], are well-suited for identifying and localizing multiple cell instances in a single complex microscopic image. However, training these models effectively requires vast, homogeneous, and meticulously annotated datasets, a challenge compounded by the natural heterogeneity of image acquisition conditions across clinical labs.

Report issue for preceding element

To address this, the work introduces a comprehensive, end-to-end deep learning framework, emphasizing two core contributions:

Report issue for preceding element

1. 1.


A rigorous data standardization and merging pipeline designed to consolidate four public blood cell datasets (PBC, BCCD, Chula, Sickle Cell) into a unified, conflict-free training corpus, mitigating the problem of data scarcity.

Report issue for preceding element

2. 2.


A comparative experimental study utilizing the Faster R-CNN model with a ResNet-50-FPN backbone to evaluate the crucial impact of Transfer Learning (pre-trained on Microsoft COCO\[ [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib9 "")\]) versus random initialization (Baseline) on model performance and convergence speed.

Report issue for preceding element


The remaining sections of this paper are structured as follows: Section II reviews related work in blood cell image analysis. Section III details the full technical methodology, including data processing, merging, and model architecture. Section IV presents the comparative results and qualitative detection analysis. Finally, Section V concludes the paper and outlines areas for future research.

Report issue for preceding element

## II Related Work

Report issue for preceding element

The automated analysis of blood cell images has seen significant advancements, largely driven by the convergence of public datasets and powerful deep learning architectures. Research efforts in this domain can be broadly categorized by their focus on data resources, specific segmentation techniques, and classification methodologies. This review situates the work within these key areas.

Report issue for preceding element

### II-A Data Resources and Benchmarking

Report issue for preceding element

The integrity and generalizability of deep learning models for hematology rely fundamentally on access to high-quality, annotated data. Initial benchmarking efforts frequently utilized foundational resources, such as the BCCD Dataset \[ [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib4 "")\], which established early standards for classification and detection tasks.

Report issue for preceding element

Current research addresses the need for comprehensive and diverse training data through sophisticated curation processes. For instance, Gan et al. \[ [5](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib5 "")\] have introduced a curated and re-annotated Peripheral Blood Cell Dataset (PBCD). This resource integrates four distinct public datasets, aiming to enhance the richness and uniformity of annotations to ultimately improve model robustness and generalizability. This highlights a clear trend toward data aggregation, which the work adopts and extends. While Gan et al. \[ [5](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib5 "")\] focused on the curation itself, the paper presents a fully transparent and reproducible data-merging pipeline (Algorithms 1 and 2) and, critically, uses this new unified corpus to perform a rigorous comparative study of transfer learning versus a baseline model.

Report issue for preceding element

### II-B Segmentation Techniques

Report issue for preceding element

Accurate cell segmentation remains a critical and complex challenge, particularly for Red Blood Cells (RBCs), due to the frequent presence of overlapping cells in typical blood smear preparations. Naruenatthanaset et al. \[ [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib1 "")\] proposed a targeted solution by developing an RBC segmentation methodology that explicitly incorporates techniques for separating these challenging overlapping clusters. Furthermore, their work addresses the issue of classification in imbalanced datasets, a challenge the merged dataset also faces.

Report issue for preceding element

This focus on overlapping clusters is vital. The work addresses this same problem, not through a multi-stage segmentation-then-classification pipeline, but by employing a modern object detection framework. The Faster R-CNN architecture is inherently designed to manage this challenge by using a Region Proposal Network (RPN) to identify and localize multiple, potentially overlapping, object instances in a single pass.

Report issue for preceding element

### II-C Classification Models and Applications

Report issue for preceding element

Classification of the various peripheral blood cell types, including White Blood Cells (WBCs), RBCs, and platelets, is predominantly executed using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). Acevedo et al. \[ [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib2 "")\] demonstrated the high efficacy of CNNs for peripheral blood cell image recognition, often utilizing fine-tuning and transfer learning to adapt deep network weights to the microscopic domain. This strategy of using pre-trained models was also investigated by Alkafrawi and Dakhell \[ [7](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib7 "")\], who successfully applied models such as AlexNet for blood cell classification.

Report issue for preceding element

While effective for single-cell cropped images, these classification-based approaches are less efficient for analyzing complex, whole-slide-style images. The work therefore adopts a more robust and scalable object detection paradigm. By implementing the Faster R-CNN framework with a ResNet-50-FPN backbone, the methodology can identify, classify, and localize all cell instances in a single, end-to-end-trainable model. This comprehensive detection approach also allows the model to learn features for specific, high-priority diagnostic applications, such as the detection of sickle cells, a task investigated in a dedicated method by Tushabe et al. \[ [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib3 "")\].

Report issue for preceding element

Finally, the research field is supported by focused systematic reviews, such as the one by Asghar et al. \[ [8](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib8 "")\], which provides an exhaustive analysis of ML and DL models exclusively applied to the classification of White Blood Cells (WBCs). The methodology is informed by these reviews, but the experimental study provides a novel contribution by evaluating a state-of-the-art detection model’s performance across a uniquely aggregated dataset of 25 distinct cell classes, moving beyond just WBCs.

Report issue for preceding element

## III Methodology

Report issue for preceding element

The objective of this work is to develop a highly robust and generalizable object detection model for blood cell classification by leveraging a large, curated, multi-source dataset. The proposed methodology encompasses a rigorous data processing pipeline to standardize disparate datasets, followed by training a state-of-the-art object detection network using optimized parameters. The entire deep learning framework was built upon the PyTorch platform \[ [12](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib12 "")\].

Report issue for preceding element

### III-A Data Acquisition and Standardization

Report issue for preceding element

Four distinct public datasets were utilized: the PBC dataset\[ [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib2 "")\], the BCCD dataset\[ [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib4 "")\], a dataset on Sickle Cell detection\[ [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib3 "")\], and the Chula dataset\[ [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib1 "")\]. To mitigate heterogeneity and ensure compatibility for centralized training, a standardized preprocessing pipeline was applied to each source. This pipeline performs image scaling and standardized bounding box generation using Algorithm [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#alg1 "Algorithm 1 ‣ III-A Data Acquisition and Standardization ‣ III Methodology ‣ Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN"). The output of this stage is a collection of standardized images, bounding box annotations, and metadata files for each dataset.

Report issue for preceding element

### III-B Multi-Source Dataset Merging

Report issue for preceding element

The standardized datasets were subsequently merged into a single comprehensive training resource, Merging, using Algorithm [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#alg2 "Algorithm 2 ‣ III-B Multi-Source Dataset Merging ‣ III Methodology ‣ Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN"). This step involved sequential renaming of all image files to ensure global uniqueness, aggregation of annotation records using the Pandas library, and consolidation of metadata. A unique, sequential integer ID was assigned to each image, replacing the original source filename to create a robust and conflict-free master dataset.

Report issue for preceding element

### III-C Unified Dataset Characteristics

Report issue for preceding element

After merging, the final unified training dataset consisted of 19470 images and 46332 total bounding box annotations. The contribution from each source dataset is detailed in Table [I](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#S3.T1 "TABLE I ‣ III-C Unified Dataset Characteristics ‣ III Methodology ‣ Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN"). During preprocessing, the ”wbc (general)” class, which contained only 2 instances, was identified as a data-merging artifact and removed from the dataset. This class was statistically insufficient for training and would not have contributed to the model’s performance. This merging process resulted in a dataset with significant class imbalance, which was a primary challenge for the model.

Report issue for preceding element

While common classes like lymphocyte and neutrophil were well-represented (over 1000 instances each), many critical classes identified in Section IV-B were extremely rare. For example, the final dataset contained fewer than 1221 instances of basophil and 307 instances of teardrop, directly explaining the low mAP scores achieved for these classes.

Report issue for preceding element

### III-D Object Detection Model Architecture

Report issue for preceding element

The core detection model is the Faster R-CNN\[ [10](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib10 "")\] framework. Its architecture is specifically tailored for high-accuracy object detection in complex visual environments, such as microscopic imagery. The detailed component configuration is summarized in Table [II](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#S3.T2 "TABLE II ‣ III-D Object Detection Model Architecture ‣ III Methodology ‣ Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN").

Report issue for preceding element

TABLE II: Faster R-CNN Model Architecture Overview

| Component | Configuration and Role |
| --- | --- |
| Detection Framework | Faster R-CNN\[ [10](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib10 "")\] (Implemented in PyTorch\[ [12](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib12 "")\]) |
| Backbone | ResNet-50 (Residual Network). Serves as the feature extractor from input images. |
| Feature Aggregation | Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)\[ [11](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib11 "")\]. Enhances feature maps by combining low-resolution, high-semantic information with high-resolution, low-semantic information. |
| Pre-training (Regimen 2) | Microsoft COCO\[ [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib9 "")\] weights. Used to initialize the backbone for transfer learning. |
| Output Head | FastRCNNPredictor. Replaced the standard head to output a customized number of classes. |
| Output Classes | Nc​l​a​s​s​e​s=25N\_{classes}=25 (24 blood cell classes + 1 mandatory background class). |

Report issue for preceding element

### III-E Experimental Regimens and Training Strategies

Report issue for preceding element

The deep learning model was implemented using the PyTorch framework, and experiments were conducted across two distinct regimens to thoroughly evaluate the impact of initialization and preprocessing on convergence and final performance. The combined dataset was randomly partitioned into a training set (90%) and a validation set (10%). Training utilized the Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimizer (lr=0.005\\textbf{lr}=0.005, momentum=0.9\\textbf{momentum}=0.9, weight\_decay=0.0005\\textbf{weight\\\_decay}=0.0005) and was run for 25 epochs with a StepLR scheduler (γ=0.1\\gamma=0.1, step\_size=7\\textbf{step\\\_size}=7).

Report issue for preceding element

#### III-E1 Regimen 1: Baseline (Random Initialization)

Report issue for preceding element

The baseline approach utilized an Faster R-CNN model with randomly initialized weights (i.e., no external pre-training). The data pipeline incorporated a custom preprocessing pipeline designed to enhance microscopic features, including explicit image adjustments such as contrast/brightness adjustments and Unsharp Mask filtering before converting the images to tensors and applying per-channel normalization. This regimen aims to establish a baseline performance without the benefit of prior knowledge from natural image domains.

Report issue for preceding element

#### III-E2 Regimen 2: Transfer Learning (Primary)

Report issue for preceding element

The primary regimen leverages Transfer Learning by initializing the ResNet-50-FPN backbone with weights pre-trained on the comprehensive Microsoft COCO dataset\[ [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib9 "")\]. This strategy, leveraging features learned from a large, general-purpose image collection, significantly accelerates training convergence and enhances initial feature representation learning. The data pipeline adheres to the standard conventions for pre-trained models: images are transformed using T.C​o​m​p​o​s​eT.Compose, incorporating data augmentation (Random Horizontal Flip, Color Jitter), followed by Normalization with ImageNet channel means (μ=\[0.485,0.456,0.406\]\\mu=\[0.485,0.456,0.406\]) and standard deviations (σ=\[0.229,0.224,0.225\]\\sigma=\[0.229,0.224,0.225\]). Gradient clipping with a maximum norm of 1.01.0 was applied during backpropagation to enhance numerical stability.

Report issue for preceding element

## IV Results and Discussion

Report issue for preceding element

The performance of the two experimental regimens was evaluated quantitatively using standard object detection metrics, followed by an analysis of loss convergence and qualitative prediction samples.

Report issue for preceding element

### IV-A Quantitative Performance Metrics

Report issue for preceding element

To rigorously assess detection accuracy, standard Mean Average Precision (mAP) metrics was computed, as defined by the COCO and PASCAL VOC challenges \[ [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib9 ""), [13](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib13 "")\]. The primary metric, mAP (IoU .50:.95), provides a comprehensive score by averaging precision across ten Intersection over Union (IoU) thresholds (from 0.50 to 0.95). Also report mAP@.50 (the PASCAL VOC standard) and mAP@.75 (a stricter localization metric).

Report issue for preceding element

The results, summarized in Table [III](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#S4.T3 "TABLE III ‣ IV-A Quantitative Performance Metrics ‣ IV Results and Discussion ‣ Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN"), demonstrate a clear quantitative advantage for the Transfer Learning approach.

Report issue for preceding element

TABLE III: Comparative Model Performance Metrics: Baseline vs. Transfer Learning

| Metric | Regimen 1 (Baseline) | Regimen 2 (Transfer) |
| mAP (IoU 50:.95) | 0.2618 | 0.2832 |
| mAP@.50 | 0.4437 | 0.4476 |
| mAP@.75 | 0.2911 | 0.3316 |
| mAP (small objects) | 0.1935 | 0.1866 |
| mAP (medium objects) | 0.1490 | 0.1578 |
| mAP (large objects) | 0.5113 | 0.5618 |
| mAR (100 Detections) | 0.3989 | 0.4133 |

Report issue for preceding element

Regimen 2 (Transfer Learning) outperformed the baseline in every primary metric. Notably, the largest performance gap was in mAP​@​.75\\text{mAP}@.75 (0.3316 vs. 0.2911), suggesting that the COCO pre-trained features provided the model with a superior ability to achieve precise bounding box localization. Furthermore, Regimen 2 showed significant gains in detecting large objects (mAP (large) 0.5618 vs. 0.5113), indicating that transfer learning enhances the detection of prominent cells in the field of view. While the mAP​@​.50\\text{mAP}@.50 scores were nearly identical, the higher overall mAP and mAR (Mean Average Recall) confirm the Transfer Learning model is more accurate and robust.

Report issue for preceding element

While Regimen 2 (Transfer Learning) outperformed the baseline in every primary metric, it is important to acknowledge a limitation in this comparative study. The two experimental regimens utilized different data processing pipelines; the baseline (Regimen 1) incorporated a custom preprocessing pipeline with image adjustments such as Unsharp Mask filtering , whereas the transfer learning (Regimen 2) model’s pipeline included standard data augmentations like Random Horizontal Flip and Color Jitter.

Report issue for preceding element

This difference introduces a confounding variable. The superior performance of Regimen 2, while strongly suggesting the benefit of COCO pre-trained weights, may also be partially attributed to its more robust data augmentation strategy. Future work should aim to isolate these variables by evaluating both randomly initialized and pre-trained models under an identical augmentation pipeline.

Report issue for preceding element

| teardrop | 0.0000 | 0.0105 | +0.0105 | microcyte | 0.0069 | 0.0000 | -0.0069 |
| burr\_cell | 0.0000 | 0.0053 | +0.0053 | hypochromia | 0.0031 | 0.0425 | +0.0394 |
| ovalocyte | 0.0089 | 0.0044 | -0.0045 | stomatocyte | 0.0517 | 0.0356 | -0.0161 |
| basophil | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | uncategorized | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |

Report issue for preceding element

- •


Strong Performance: Both models were effective at identifying common White Blood Cells (WBCs). For example, Regimen 2 achieved mAP@.50 scores of 0.7023 for lymphocyte, 0.6759 for neutrophil, and 0.6797 for eosinophil.

Report issue for preceding element

- •


Weak Performance: Both regimens struggled with rare cell types and those defined by subtle morphological changes. For instance, sickle\_cell (0.1140 vs 0.0702) and schistocyte (0.0769 vs 0.1033) had very low mAP scores.

Report issue for preceding element

- •


Critical Failures: Both models achieved an mAP of 0.0 for several classes, including basophil, wbc (a general class), teardrop, and uncategorized. This strongly indicates a critical data scarcity for these specific classes within the merged dataset, presenting a clear direction for future work in targeted data augmentation.

Report issue for preceding element


### IV-C Quantifying and Addressing Critical Class Imbalance

Report issue for preceding element

While the dataset merging pipeline successfully consolidated 46,332 bounding box annotations from four sources \[ [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib1 ""), [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib2 ""), [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib3 ""), [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib4 "")\], the per-class analysis revealed that aggregation alone is insufficient to address the extreme heterogeneity in diagnostic significance. The model’s complete failure (mAP of 0.0) on classes such as basophil, teardrop, and uncategorized is a direct consequence of this severe class imbalance.

Report issue for preceding element

To better quantify this limitation, Table V details the instance counts for the critically rare cell classes that demonstrated mAP​@​.50\\text{mAP}@.50 scores of near zero.

Report issue for preceding element

TABLE V: Instance Count for Critically Rare Cell Classes

| Cell Class | Total Instances | mAP@.50 (R2) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| basophil | 1221 | 0.0000 |
| uncategorized | 184 | 0.0000 |
| microcyte | 481 | 0.0000 |
| burr\_cell | 785 | 0.0053 |
| teardrop | 307 | 0.0105 |

Report issue for preceding element

Future Mitigation Strategies. This structural limitation in the training data must be the primary focus of future research. Specifically, the following strategies are warranted to elevate the performance floor for diagnostically critical, rare classes:

Report issue for preceding element

- •


Targeted Data Augmentation: Implementing advanced, class-specific augmentation techniques, such as geometric and photometric transformations tailored to the morphology of basophils, to synthesize new training samples.

Report issue for preceding element

- •


Synthetic Data Generation: Employing Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) or diffusion models, conditioned on the rare cell images, to generate high-fidelity, labeled synthetic instances to supplement the sparse real data.

Report issue for preceding element

- •


Few-Shot and Active Learning: Utilizing few-shot learning approaches that can learn robust representations from the limited existing samples, or implementing an active learning pipeline to prioritize the acquisition and manual annotation of images containing these rare cell types.

Report issue for preceding element


This redirection from simple data aggregation to intelligent, targeted data enrichment is essential to transform the current framework into a high-accuracy, deployable diagnostic tool across the full spectrum of hematological disorders.

Report issue for preceding element

### IV-D Training Loss and Model Convergence

Report issue for preceding element

The analysis of training and validation loss supports the quantitative mAP findings. The Faster R-CNN model utilizes a multi-task loss function that is the sum of four contributing losses: two for the Region Proposal Network (RPN) and two for the final Fast R-CNN Head\[ [10](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib10 "")\].

Report issue for preceding element

#### IV-D1 Regimen 1: Baseline Performance

Report issue for preceding element

The model trained from random initialization (Regimen 1) showed slower convergence, as detailed in Table [VI](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#S4.T6 "TABLE VI ‣ IV-D1 Regimen 1: Baseline Performance ‣ IV-D Training Loss and Model Convergence ‣ IV Results and Discussion ‣ Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN"). The initial training loss was high (∼0.160\\sim 0.160) and the final validation loss of 0.10756 reflects the model’s difficulty in learning complex features from scratch.

Report issue for preceding element

TABLE VI: Regimen 1 Loss Profile: Baseline Model (Random Weights)

| Epoch | Training Loss | Validation Loss |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 0.15953 | 0.12298 |
| 5 | 0.11450 | 0.10834 |
| 10 | 0.10704 | 0.10549 |
| 15 | 0.09882 | 0.10232 |
| 20 | 0.09995 | 0.09595 |
| 25 | 0.10611 | 0.10756 |

Report issue for preceding element

#### IV-D2 Regimen 2: Transfer Learning Performance

Report issue for preceding element

The model initialized with COCO pre-trained weights (Regimen 2) demonstrated markedly improved performance (see Table [VII](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#S4.T7 "TABLE VII ‣ IV-D2 Regimen 2: Transfer Learning Performance ‣ IV-D Training Loss and Model Convergence ‣ IV Results and Discussion ‣ Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN")). The initial training loss (0.10625) was already lower than the final loss of Regimen 1, demonstrating instant feature utility. The model quickly stabilized, achieving a final validation loss of 0.08666. This lower loss, which correlates directly with the higher mAP scores, validates the strategy of incorporating external feature knowledge.

Report issue for preceding element

TABLE VII: Regimen 2 Loss Profile: Transfer Learning Model (COCO Pre-trained)

| Epoch | Training Loss | Validation Loss |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 0.10625 | 0.09845 |
| 5 | 0.09987 | 0.10290 |
| 10 | 0.08577 | 0.09094 |
| 15 | 0.08111 | 0.08686 |
| 20 | 0.08122 | 0.08713 |
| 25 | 0.08093 | 0.08666 |

Report issue for preceding element![Refer to caption](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1/Loss_graph.png)Figure 1: Loss Convergence Comparison. A plot showing the averaged training and validation loss over 25 epochs for both Regimen 1 (Baseline) and Regimen 2 (Transfer Learning). The figure demonstrates the rapid convergence and superior stability achieved by the Transfer Learning Regimen.Report issue for preceding element

### IV-E Qualitative Prediction Analysis

Report issue for preceding element

Qualitative analysis of the model’s predictions (Figure [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#S4.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ IV-E Qualitative Prediction Analysis ‣ IV Results and Discussion ‣ Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN")) complements the quantitative metrics. Visualizations confirmed the Transfer Learning model’s efficacy across various cell types and imaging conditions.

Report issue for preceding element

![Refer to caption](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1/Comparison_033474.jpg)(a)PBC Dataset SampleReport issue for preceding element

![Refer to caption](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1/Comparison_000001.jpg)(b)BCCD Dataset SampleReport issue for preceding element

![Refer to caption](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1/Comparison_001161.jpg)(c)Sickle Cell SampleReport issue for preceding element

Figure 2: Qualitative Detection Results: Actual vs. Predicted.
Detection outputs from the Transfer Learning model (Regimen 2) across datasets, demonstrating accurate localization and classification.
(A) PBC Dataset, (B) BCCD Dataset, (C) Sickle Cell Sample.Report issue for preceding element

Visual inspection of predictions showed high overlap between ground truth bounding boxes and predicted boxes across all four source datasets. Specifically, the model demonstrated robust detection of complex shapes like sickle cells and accurate segregation of overlapping cell clusters (a known challenge in hematology). This qualitative success, now supported by the formal mAP metrics, confirms the model’s viability.

Report issue for preceding element

### IV-F Failure Case Analysis

Report issue for preceding element

To better understand the model’s limitations, a qualitative analysis of common failure modes was conducted. As expected from the quantitative metrics in Section IV-B, the model’s failures were not random but fell into predictable categories, primarily driven by data scarcity and visual complexity.

Report issue for preceding element

- •


False Negatives on Rare Classes: This was the most common failure for classes with 0.0 mAP, such as basophil. As shown in Fig. [3(a)](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#S4.F3.sf1 "In Figure 3 ‣ IV-F Failure Case Analysis ‣ IV Results and Discussion ‣ Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN"), the model simply fails to propose any region for the target cell, despite it being clearly visible. This is a classic symptom of extreme class imbalance in the training data.

Report issue for preceding element

- •


Localization Errors in Dense Clusters: The model also struggled in regions with many overlapping cells. This often resulted in either merging multiple objects (e.g., two RBCs) into a single incorrect bounding box (Fig. [3(b)](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#S4.F3.sf2 "In Figure 3 ‣ IV-F Failure Case Analysis ‣ IV Results and Discussion ‣ Generalizable Blood Cell Detection via Unified Dataset and Faster R-CNN")) or, in some cases, failing to make any prediction at all, as the dense cluster was suppressed as ’background’.

Report issue for preceding element


These failures underscore the findings from the per-class analysis: the primary limitation of the current framework is not the model architecture, but the severe class imbalance and visual complexity in the underlying data.

Report issue for preceding element

![Refer to caption](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1/Inference_Outputs/Overlay_001502.jpg)(a)Missed basophil (False Negative). The Ground Truth box is shown, but the model made no prediction.Report issue for preceding element

![Refer to caption](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1/Inference_Outputs/Overlay_000414.jpg)(b)Localization Error. The model predicted one large box for two distinct, overlapping RBCs.Report issue for preceding element

Figure 3: Qualitative Failure Case Analysis (Regimen 2).
Examples of common prediction errors. Visualization for Ground Truth in Green and Model Predictions in Red.Report issue for preceding element

## V CONCLUSION

Report issue for preceding element

This work implemented and validated a comprehensive methodology for automated peripheral blood cell detection by training a Faster R-CNN framework \[ [10](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib10 "")\] on a consolidated multi-source dataset. The critical data standardization and merging pipelines effectively unified heterogeneous data from four public repositories: PBC \[ [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib2 "")\], BCCD \[ [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib4 "")\], Chula \[ [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib1 "")\], and Sickle Cell \[ [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib3 "")\].

Report issue for preceding element

The comparative study rigorously demonstrated the efficacy of transfer learning. The Regimen 2 (Transfer Learning) model, initialized with Microsoft COCO weights \[ [9](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08465v1#bib.bib9 "")\], achieved a superior mAP​@​.75\\text{mAP}@.75 of 0.3316 (compared to 0.2911 for the baseline) and a more stable final validation loss of 0.08666 (compared to 0.10756). This validates COCO pre-training as a superior strategy for accelerating feature extraction in this specialized domain, especially for improving localization accuracy.

Report issue for preceding element

However, the per-class analysis exposed a critical limitation: both models failed completely on several rare classes (e.g., basophil, uncategorized), achieving 0.0 mAP. This finding is significant, as it shows that simply merging datasets is insufficient to solve severe class imbalance. Future research must move beyond simple aggregation and focus on targeted data augmentation, few-shot learning, or synthetic data generation to address this fundamental challenge in automated hematological analysis.

Report issue for preceding element

# SBCW-YOLO: A High-Precision, and Lightweight Model for Blood Cell Detection

22 PagesPosted: 16 Dec 2025

[See all articles by Chuanhao Duanmu](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/cf_dev/AbsByAuth.cfm?per_id=9448472 "View other papers by this author")

## [Chuanhao Duanmu](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/cf_dev/AbsByAuth.cfm?per_id=9448472 "View other papers by this author")

Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications

## [Kexue Sun](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/cf_dev/AbsByAuth.cfm?per_id=9448473 "View other papers by this author")

Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications

## [Yuheng Zha](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/cf_dev/AbsByAuth.cfm?per_id=9587617 "View other papers by this author")

Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications

### Abstract

Blood cell recognition and detection algorithms currently face significant challenges, notably unbalanced detection accuracy for multi-scale blood cells and the difficulty of simultaneously achieving lightweight design and high precision. To address these issues, this paper proposes an improved blood cell detection algorithm based on YOLOv11n, designated as SBCW-YOLO. This algorithm breaks through performance bottlenecks through four core optimizations: adopting a "Scale-Customized Attention" strategy to improve the detection accuracy of blood cells of different scales; introducing the BiFPN (Bidirectional Feature Pyramid Network) structure to enhance multi-scale feature interaction; using the C3k2\_Faster\_EMA module to balance feature extraction and lightweight design; and proposing the Wise-Inner-MPDIoU loss to optimize bounding box regression. Experiments conducted on the BCCD dataset demonstrate the excellent performance of SBCW-YOLO: the mAP50 reaches 94.3%, with an AP of 88.8% for red blood cells and 94.6% for platelets. Compared with the original YOLOv11n, the mAP50 is improved by 2.3%, while the number of parameters is reduced by 35% and the computational complexity is decreased by 8%. Experiments show that SBCW-YOLO outperforms YOLOv11 and other mainstream models, meets the requirements of lightweight and high-precision object detection, and provides a reference scheme for clinical blood cell detection.

**Keywords:** Blood Cell Detection, YOLO, Attention mechanism, BiFPN, Wise-Inner-MPDIoU
